skill.name: prompt-engineering
skill.description: Guide for designing, testing, and refining prompts with examples, templates, reasoning patterns, and prompt evaluation practices.
skill.summary: Build clearer prompts using examples, constraints, templates, and iterative evaluation.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported Ruby shell backtick execution and weak cryptographic algorithm patterns, but the reviewed locations are Markdown prose or fenced prompt examples. No executable scripts, shell invocations, cryptographic APIs, data exfiltration behavior, or audit-bypass instructions were found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static external command findings point to Markdown code fences and prompt examples, not runnable Ruby or shell backtick execution. No command execution API or shell command is present at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are documentation examples in SKILL.md. They do not contain executable backtick substitution, shell commands, or code that would run commands.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Crypto Pattern Matches in Prose
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm findings occur in descriptive text and prompt examples. No hashing function, encryption routine, credential handling, or cryptographic dependency appears at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain skill metadata, prompt engineering guidance, headings, or example text. There is no semantic evidence of weak cryptography use.

low_findings.title: Benign System Prompt Example Requires Context Isolation
low_findings.description: SKILL.md includes a fenced example beginning with a system prompt for teaching prompt design. It does not ask the evaluator to ignore instructions, change risk levels, or bypass review, but it should be treated as untrusted skill content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example uses system-prompt wording that can resemble prompt injection patterns. Context shows it is an educational example, not an attempted override.

content.user_title: Improve Prompts with Proven LLM Patterns
content.value_statement: Many prompts fail because goals, examples, and output rules are unclear. This skill provides practical patterns for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains few-shot prompting with input and output examples.
Shows how to structure prompts for multi-step reasoning tasks.
Provides a reusable prompt template pattern with variables.
Describes system prompt design for stable model behavior.
Lists common prompt failures such as ambiguity and context overflow.
Recommends iterative testing for accuracy, consistency, and token use.
limitations: It is a guidance-only skill and does not run automated prompt tests.
It does not include benchmark datasets or evaluation scripts.
It cannot guarantee better model output without representative test cases.
It does not provide model-specific API configuration guidance.

use_cases.title: Standardize support ticket extraction
use_cases.description: Create consistent prompts that classify issues, priority, and missing information from customer tickets.

use_cases.title: Improve agent debugging sessions
use_cases.description: Refine prompts that help agents analyze failures, isolate root causes, and report uncertainty clearly.

use_cases.title: Build reusable team prompt templates
use_cases.description: Turn repeated writing, review, and analysis tasks into structured prompts with variables and output rules.

prompt_templates.title: Clarify a Basic Prompt
prompt_templates.prompt: Improve this prompt for clarity and consistency. Keep the original goal, add missing constraints, and define the expected output format: [paste prompt].

prompt_templates.title: Add Few-Shot Examples
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a few-shot prompt for this task: [task]. Include three representative examples, one edge case, and a concise instruction before the new input.

prompt_templates.title: Debug Agent Behavior
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze why this prompt produced the wrong behavior. Identify ambiguous instructions, missing context, conflicting goals, and a revised prompt: [prompt and output].

prompt_templates.title: Design a Production Prompt System
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a reusable prompt system for [workflow]. Include system guidance, variable sections, evaluation criteria, fallback behavior, and versioning recommendations.

output_examples.input: A vague prompt asks the model to summarize an article.
output_examples.output: A clearer prompt defines audience, length, focus areas, missing-information behavior, and a concise bullet format.

output_examples.input: An agent gives inconsistent code review feedback across files.
output_examples.output: A structured review prompt separates scope, checklist, severity labels, examples of good findings, and final reporting format.

output_examples.input: A team repeats the same analysis prompt in many projects.
output_examples.output: A reusable template captures stable instructions once and leaves project name, source material, and output requirements as variables.

best_practices: Start with a simple prompt, then add constraints only when tests show a need.
Use representative examples that match the real inputs and expected outputs.
Track prompt versions, expected behavior, and evaluation results together.
anti_patterns: Do not hide conflicting goals inside long instructions.
Do not add many examples before testing whether a direct instruction works.
Do not rely on reasoning prompts when the output format is undefined.

faq.question: What does this skill help me do?
faq.answer: It helps you design clearer prompts, add useful examples, and refine instructions for more reliable LLM output.

faq.question: Does it execute code or call external services?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed skill is documentation-only and contains no executable scripts or network calls.

faq.question: Can it improve prompts for Claude and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The patterns are general and can be applied to Claude, Codex, Claude Code, and other LLM tools.

faq.question: When should I use few-shot prompting?
faq.answer: Use it when examples make the desired format or reasoning pattern clearer than rules alone.

faq.question: What should I test after changing a prompt?
faq.answer: Test accuracy, consistency, token use, edge cases, and how the model handles missing information.

faq.question: Is this a prompt evaluation framework?
faq.answer: No. It explains evaluation practices, but it does not include automated scoring tools or datasets.
